In cases involving purchasing goods, many organizations use a certain monetary threshold above which a ''Ringi-sho'' is required. Additionally, ''Ringi-sho'' are used for a wide range of organizational approvals, such as [[personnel changes]], promotions, and contract agreements. The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * [[Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ringi-sho}} [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] [[Category:稟議書]][[Category:Approval documents]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Approval documents-qq]] [[Category:Approval documents]][[Category:Approval documents]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Approval documents-qq]] [[Category:Approval documents]][[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:稟議書]]

{{Unreferenced|date=August 2020}} [[File:Official document (S39).jpg|thumb|right|250px|A ''Ringi-sho'' (proposal document) created by the Ministry of International Trade and Industry. Many individuals stamped their approval before it reached the final decision-maker, the minister.]] A '''''Ringi-sho''''' (稟議書)According to [[Kōjien]] and [[Daijirin]], the reading of "稟議書" is "Ringi-sho." However, various Japanese dictionaries classify "Ringi" as a [[Hyakushō-yomi|popular reading]] (''hyakushō-yomi'') of "Hingi." is a document created for the purpose of conducting ''[[Ringi]]'' (consultative decision-making). In [[government agencies]], it is often referred to as a '''''Kiansho''''' (起案書), while in corporate settings, alternative terms like '''''Ringi-sho,'' ''Kiansho,'' and ''Ritsuansho''''' may also be used. == Overview == A ''Ringi-sho'' is a document used for conducting ''[[Ringi]]'', a method within organizations such as [[companies]] and government agencies to reduce the time spent convening [[meetings]]. In this method, the person in charge drafts a proposal for a straightforward matter and circulates it among stakeholders to obtain their seals or signatures of approval. In principle, organizational decisions are made through a [[consensus decision-making]] process like meetings. For instance, decisions about executing business operations, such as those made by a [[board of directors]], must be performed by the company's directors themselves, and circular resolutions are only allowed exceptionally. However, meetings consume time and costs, so day-to-day decisions and simpler approvals are often delegated to designated decision-makers. Nonetheless, entrusting all judgment to the final decision-maker could risk neglecting due diligence on individual cases. Furthermore, securing approval from involved parties before making a decision ensures smooth execution post-decision. ''Ringi'' facilitates a broader and more cautious consideration by involving multiple stakeholders before the final decision-maker grants approval. Additionally, documenting the content and process of organizational decision-making in written form is desirable not only for operational convenience but also to make auditing and external review easier. When decisions are made in meetings, meeting minutes or summaries of resolutions are created and recorded. Conversely, when decisions are made via ''Ringi'', the person initiating the proposal prepares a document detailing the proposed decision, which is circulated for stakeholders’ approvals, eventually forming the ''Ringi-sho''. The final decision-maker confirms stakeholders' approvals before authorizing the proposal, completing the organization's decision-making process. This method is particularly suited for decision-making in relatively large organizations. == Format == The format of a ''Ringi-sho'' varies between organizations. It is often an internal document, with each organization establishing its own format. Below is an example: {{Indent| (Title) Regarding the purchase of a company vehicle
(Body) Concerning the above matter, I propose the purchase of a vehicle as outlined below:
Details
# Reason: The delivery vehicle has deteriorated, necessitating a replacement.
# Cost: '''*,000 yen }} Additional details such as the vendor's name, the budget allocated (e.g., ''' yen), actual expenses (e.g., ''' yen), and any differences are typically included, along with an attached quotation. In cases involving purchasing goods, many organizations use a certain monetary threshold above which a ''Ringi-sho'' is required. Additionally, ''Ringi-sho'' are used for a wide range of organizational approvals, such as [[personnel changes]], promotions, and contract agreements. The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * [[Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ringi-sho}} [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] [[Category:稟議書]][[Category:Approval documents]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Approval documents-qq]] [[Category:Approval documents]][[Category:Approval documents]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Approval documents-qq]] [[Category:Approval documents]][[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:稟議書]]

{{Unreferenced|date=August 2020}} [[File:Official document (S39).jpg|thumb|right|250px|A ''Ringi-sho'' (proposal document) created by the Ministry of International Trade and Industry. Many individuals stamped their approval before it reached the final decision-maker, the minister.]] A '''''Ringi-sho''''' (稟議書)According to [[Kōjien]] and [[Daijirin]], the reading of "稟議書" is "Ringi-sho." However, various Japanese dictionaries classify "Ringi" as a [[Hyakushō-yomi|popular reading]] (''hyakushō-yomi'') of "Hingi." is a document created for the purpose of conducting ''[[Ringi]]'' (consultative decision-making). In [[government agencies]], it is often referred to as a '''''Kiansho''''' (起案書), while in corporate settings, alternative terms like '''''Ringi-sho,'' ''Kiansho,'' and ''Ritsuansho''''' may also be used. == Overview == A ''Ringi-sho'' is a document used for conducting ''[[Ringi]]'', a method within organizations such as [[companies]] and government agencies to reduce the time spent convening [[meetings]]. In this method, the person in charge drafts a proposal for a straightforward matter and circulates it among stakeholders to obtain their seals or signatures of approval. In principle, organizational decisions are made through a [[consensus decision-making]] process like meetings. For instance, decisions about executing business operations, such as those made by a [[board of directors]], must be performed by the company's directors themselves, and circular resolutions are only allowed exceptionally. However, meetings consume time and costs, so day-to-day decisions and simpler approvals are often delegated to designated decision-makers. Nonetheless, entrusting all judgment to the final decision-maker could risk neglecting due diligence on individual cases. Furthermore, securing approval from involved parties before making a decision ensures smooth execution post-decision. ''Ringi'' facilitates a broader and more cautious consideration by involving multiple stakeholders before the final decision-maker grants approval. Additionally, documenting the content and process of organizational decision-making in written form is desirable not only for operational convenience but also to make auditing and external review easier. When decisions are made in meetings, meeting minutes or summaries of resolutions are created and recorded. Conversely, when decisions are made via ''Ringi'', the person initiating the proposal prepares a document detailing the proposed decision, which is circulated for stakeholders’ approvals, eventually forming the ''Ringi-sho''. The final decision-maker confirms stakeholders' approvals before authorizing the proposal, completing the organization's decision-making process. This method is particularly suited for decision-making in relatively large organizations. == Format == The format of a ''Ringi-sho'' varies between organizations. It is often an internal document, with each organization establishing its own format. Below is an example: {{Indent| (Title) Regarding the purchase of a company vehicle
(Body) Concerning the above matter, I propose the purchase of a vehicle as outlined below:
Details
# Reason: The delivery vehicle has deteriorated, necessitating a replacement.
# Cost: '''*,000 yen }} Additional details such as the vendor's name, the budget allocated (e.g., ''' yen), actual expenses (e.g., ''' yen), and any differences are typically included, along with an attached quotation. In cases involving purchasing goods, many organizations use a certain monetary threshold above which a ''Ringi-sho'' is required. Additionally, ''Ringi-sho'' are used for a wide range of organizational approvals, such as [[personnel changes]], promotions, and contract agreements. The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * [[Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ringi-sho}} [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] [[Category:稟議書]][[Category:Approval documents]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:deprecated qq start]][[Category:deprecated qq end]] [[Category:deprecated qq start]][[Category:deprecated qq end]] [[Category:Approval documents]][[Category:Approval documents]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:deprecated qq start]][[Category:deprecated qq end]] [[Category:deprecated qq start]][[Category:deprecated qq end]] [[Category:Approval documents]][[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]] [[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]] [[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]] [[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]] [[ja:稟議書]]

{{Unreferenced|date=August 2020}} [[File:Official document (S39).jpg|thumb|right|250px|A ''Ringi-sho'' (proposal document) created by the Ministry of International Trade and Industry. Many individuals stamped their approval before it reached the final decision-maker, the minister.]] A '''''Ringi-sho''''' (稟議書)According to [[Kōjien]] and [[Daijirin]], the reading of "稟議書" is "Ringi-sho." However, various Japanese dictionaries classify "Ringi" as a [[Hyakushō-yomi|popular reading]] (''hyakushō-yomi'') of "Hingi." is a document created for the purpose of conducting ''[[Ringi]]'' (consultative decision-making). In [[government agencies]], it is often referred to as a '''''Kiansho''''' (起案書), while in corporate settings, alternative terms like '''''Ringi-sho,'' ''Kiansho,'' and ''Ritsuansho''''' may also be used. == Overview == A ''Ringi-sho'' is a document used for conducting ''[[Ringi]]'', a method within organizations such as [[companies]] and government agencies to reduce the time spent convening [[meetings]]. In this method, the person in charge drafts a proposal for a straightforward matter and circulates it among stakeholders to obtain their seals or signatures of approval. In principle, organizational decisions are made through a [[consensus decision-making]] process like meetings. For instance, decisions about executing business operations, such as those made by a [[board of directors]], must be performed by the company's directors themselves, and circular resolutions are only allowed exceptionally. However, meetings consume time and costs, so day-to-day decisions and simpler approvals are often delegated to designated decision-makers. Nonetheless, entrusting all judgment to the final decision-maker could risk neglecting due diligence on individual cases. Furthermore, securing approval from involved parties before making a decision ensures smooth execution post-decision. ''Ringi'' facilitates a broader and more cautious consideration by involving multiple stakeholders before the final decision-maker grants approval. Additionally, documenting the content and process of organizational decision-making in written form is desirable not only for operational convenience but also to make auditing and external review easier. When decisions are made in meetings, meeting minutes or summaries of resolutions are created and recorded. Conversely, when decisions are made via ''Ringi'', the person initiating the proposal prepares a document detailing the proposed decision, which is circulated for stakeholders’ approvals, eventually forming the ''Ringi-sho''. The final decision-maker confirms stakeholders' approvals before authorizing the proposal, completing the organization's decision-making process. This method is particularly suited for decision-making in relatively large organizations. == Format == The format of a ''Ringi-sho'' varies between organizations. It is often an internal document, with each organization establishing its own format. Below is an example: {{Indent| (Title) Regarding the purchase of a company vehicle
(Body) Concerning the above matter, I propose the purchase of a vehicle as outlined below:
Details
# Reason: The delivery vehicle has deteriorated, necessitating a replacement.
# Cost: '''*,000 yen }} Additional details such as the vendor's name, the budget allocated (e.g., ''' yen), actual expenses (e.g., ''' yen), and any differences are typically included, along with an attached quotation. In cases involving purchasing goods, many organizations use a certain monetary threshold above which a ''Ringi-sho'' is required. Additionally, ''Ringi-sho'' are used for a wide range of organizational approvals, such as [[personnel changes]], promotions, and contract agreements. The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * [[Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ringi-sho}} [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] [[Category:稟議書]][[Category:Approval documents]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:deprecated qq start]][[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:deprecated qq start]][[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Approval documents]][[Category:Approval documents]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:deprecated qq start]][[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:deprecated qq start]][[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Approval documents]][[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]] [[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]] [[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]] [[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]] [[ja:稟議書]]

The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * [[Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ringi-sho}} [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] [[Category:稟議書]][[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Approval documents]][[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]] [[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]] [[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]] [[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]] [[ja:稟議書]]

{{Unreferenced|date=August 2020}} [[File:Official document (S39).jpg|thumb|right|250px|A ''Ringi-sho'' (proposal document) created by the Ministry of International Trade and Industry. Many individuals stamped their approval before it reached the final decision-maker, the minister.]] A '''''Ringi-sho''''' (稟議書)According to [[Kōjien]] and [[Daijirin]], the reading of "稟議書" is "Ringi-sho." However, various Japanese dictionaries classify "Ringi" as a [[Hyakushō-yomi|popular reading]] (''hyakushō-yomi'') of "Hingi." is a document created for the purpose of conducting ''[[Ringi]]'' (consultative decision-making). In [[government agencies]], it is often referred to as a '''''Kiansho''''' (起案書), while in corporate settings, alternative terms like '''''Ringi-sho,'' ''Kiansho,'' and ''Ritsuansho''''' may also be used. == Overview == A ''Ringi-sho'' is a document used for conducting ''[[Ringi]]'', a method within organizations such as [[companies]] and government agencies to reduce the time spent convening [[meetings]]. In this method, the person in charge drafts a proposal for a straightforward matter and circulates it among stakeholders to obtain their seals or signatures of approval. In principle, organizational decisions are made through a [[consensus decision-making]] process like meetings. For instance, decisions about executing business operations, such as those made by a [[board of directors]], must be performed by the company's directors themselves, and circular resolutions are only allowed exceptionally. However, meetings consume time and costs, so day-to-day decisions and simpler approvals are often delegated to designated decision-makers. Nonetheless, entrusting all judgment to the final decision-maker could risk neglecting due diligence on individual cases. Furthermore, securing approval from involved parties before making a decision ensures smooth execution post-decision. ''Ringi'' facilitates a broader and more cautious consideration by involving multiple stakeholders before the final decision-maker grants approval. Additionally, documenting the content and process of organizational decision-making in written form is desirable not only for operational convenience but also to make auditing and external review easier. When decisions are made in meetings, meeting minutes or summaries of resolutions are created and recorded. Conversely, when decisions are made via ''Ringi'', the person initiating the proposal prepares a document detailing the proposed decision, which is circulated for stakeholders’ approvals, eventually forming the ''Ringi-sho''. The final decision-maker confirms stakeholders' approvals before authorizing the proposal, completing the organization's decision-making process. This method is particularly suited for decision-making in relatively large organizations. == Format == The format of a ''Ringi-sho'' varies between organizations. It is often an internal document, with each organization establishing its own format. Below is an example: {{Indent| (Title) Regarding the purchase of a company vehicle
(Body) Concerning the above matter, I propose the purchase of a vehicle as outlined below:
Details
# Reason: The delivery vehicle has deteriorated, necessitating a replacement.
# Cost: '''*,000 yen }} Additional details such as the vendor's name, the budget allocated (e.g., ''' yen), actual expenses (e.g., ''' yen), and any differences are typically included, along with an attached quotation. In cases involving purchasing goods, many organizations use a certain monetary threshold above which a ''Ringi-sho'' is required. Additionally, ''Ringi-sho'' are used for a wide range of organizational approvals, such as [[personnel changes]], promotions, and contract agreements. The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * [[Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ringi-sho}} [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] [[Category:稟議書]][[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:Approval documents]][[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]] [[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]] [[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]] [[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]] [[ja:稟議書]]

Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * [[Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ringi-sho}} [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] [[Category:稟議書]][[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:Approval documents]][[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]] [[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]] [[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]] [[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]] [[ja:稟議書]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]]

revision.text:
{{Unreferenced|date=August 2020}} [[File:Official document (S39).jpg|thumb|right|250px|A ''Ringi-sho'' (proposal document) created by the Ministry of International Trade and Industry. Many individuals stamped their approval before it reached the final decision-maker, the minister.]] A '''''Ringi-sho''''' (稟議書)According to [[Kōjien]] and [[Daijirin]], the reading of "稟議書" is "Ringi-sho." However, various Japanese dictionaries classify "Ringi" as a [[Hyakushō-yomi|popular reading]] (''hyakushō-yomi'') of "Hingi." is a document created for the purpose of conducting ''[[Ringi]]'' (consultative decision-making). In [[government agencies]], it is often referred to as a '''''Kiansho''''' (起案書), while in corporate settings, alternative terms like '''''Ringi-sho,'' ''Kiansho,'' and ''Ritsuansho''''' may also be used. == Overview == A ''Ringi-sho'' is a document used for conducting ''[[Ringi]]'', a method within organizations such as [[companies]] and government agencies to reduce the time spent convening [[meetings]]. In this method, the person in charge drafts a proposal for a straightforward matter and circulates it among stakeholders to obtain their seals or signatures of approval. In principle, organizational decisions are made through a [[consensus decision-making]] process like meetings. For instance, decisions about executing business operations, such as those made by a [[board of directors]], must be performed by the company's directors themselves, and circular resolutions are only allowed exceptionally. However, meetings consume time and costs, so day-to-day decisions and simpler approvals are often delegated to designated decision-makers. Nonetheless, entrusting all judgment to the final decision-maker could risk neglecting due diligence on individual cases. Furthermore, securing approval from involved parties before making a decision ensures smooth execution post-decision. ''Ringi'' facilitates a broader and more cautious consideration by involving multiple stakeholders before the final decision-maker grants approval. Additionally, documenting the content and process of organizational decision-making in written form is desirable not only for operational convenience but also to make auditing and external review easier. When decisions are made in meetings, meeting minutes or summaries of resolutions are created and recorded. Conversely, when decisions are made via ''Ringi'', the person initiating the proposal prepares a document detailing the proposed decision, which is circulated for stakeholders’ approvals, eventually forming the ''Ringi-sho''. The final decision-maker confirms stakeholders' approvals before authorizing the proposal, completing the organization's decision-making process. This method is particularly suited for decision-making in relatively large organizations. == Format == The format of a ''Ringi-sho'' varies between organizations. It is often an internal document, with each organization establishing its own format. Below is an example: {{Indent| (Title) Regarding the purchase of a company vehicle
(Body) Concerning the above matter, I propose the purchase of a vehicle as outlined below:
Details
# Reason: The delivery vehicle has deteriorated, necessitating a replacement.
# Cost: '''*,000 yen }} Additional details such as the vendor's name, the budget allocated (e.g., ''' yen), actual expenses (e.g., ''' yen), and any differences are typically included, along with an attached quotation. In cases involving purchasing goods, many organizations use a certain monetary threshold above which a ''Ringi-sho'' is required. Additionally, ''Ringi-sho'' are used for a wide range of organizational approvals, such as [[personnel changes]], promotions, and contract agreements. The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * [[Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ringi-sho}} [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] [[Category:稟議書]][[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:Approval documents]][[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]] [[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]] [[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]] [[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]][[sn:Approval documents]] [[ja:稟議書]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]]

In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * [[Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ringi-sho}} [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] [[Category:稟議書]][[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:Approval documents]][[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[ja:稟議書]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]]

Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * [[Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ringi-sho}} [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] [[Category:稟議書]][[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:Approval documents]][[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[ja:稟議書]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]]

{{Unreferenced|date=August 2020}} [[File:Official document (S39).jpg|thumb|right|250px|A ''Ringi-sho'' (proposal document) created by the Ministry of International Trade and Industry. Many individuals stamped their approval before it reached the final decision-maker, the minister.]] A '''''Ringi-sho''''' (稟議書)According to [[Kōjien]] and [[Daijirin]], the reading of "稟議書" is "Ringi-sho." However, various Japanese dictionaries classify "Ringi" as a [[Hyakushō-yomi|popular reading]] (''hyakushō-yomi'') of "Hingi." is a document created for the purpose of conducting ''[[Ringi]]'' (consultative decision-making). In [[government agencies]], it is often referred to as a '''''Kiansho''''' (起案書), while in corporate settings, alternative terms like '''''Ringi-sho,'' ''Kiansho,'' and ''Ritsuansho''''' may also be used. == Overview == A ''Ringi-sho'' is a document used for conducting ''[[Ringi]]'', a method within organizations such as [[companies]] and government agencies to reduce the time spent convening [[meetings]]. In this method, the person in charge drafts a proposal for a straightforward matter and circulates it among stakeholders to obtain their seals or signatures of approval. In principle, organizational decisions are made through a [[consensus decision-making]] process like meetings. For instance, decisions about executing business operations, such as those made by a [[board of directors]], must be performed by the company's directors themselves, and circular resolutions are only allowed exceptionally. However, meetings consume time and costs, so day-to-day decisions and simpler approvals are often delegated to designated decision-makers. Nonetheless, entrusting all judgment to the final decision-maker could risk neglecting due diligence on individual cases. Furthermore, securing approval from involved parties before making a decision ensures smooth execution post-decision. ''Ringi'' facilitates a broader and more cautious consideration by involving multiple stakeholders before the final decision-maker grants approval. Additionally, documenting the content and process of organizational decision-making in written form is desirable not only for operational convenience but also to make auditing and external review easier. When decisions are made in meetings, meeting minutes or summaries of resolutions are created and recorded. Conversely, when decisions are made via ''Ringi'', the person initiating the proposal prepares a document detailing the proposed decision, which is circulated for stakeholders’ approvals, eventually forming the ''Ringi-sho''. The final decision-maker confirms stakeholders' approvals before authorizing the proposal, completing the organization's decision-making process. This method is particularly suited for decision-making in relatively large organizations. == Format == The format of a ''Ringi-sho'' varies between organizations. It is often an internal document, with each organization establishing its own format. Below is an example: {{Indent| (Title) Regarding the purchase of a company vehicle
(Body) Concerning the above matter, I propose the purchase of a vehicle as outlined below:
Details
# Reason: The delivery vehicle has deteriorated, necessitating a replacement.
# Cost: '''*,000 yen }} Additional details such as the vendor's name, the budget allocated (e.g., ''' yen), actual expenses (e.g., ''' yen), and any differences are typically included, along with an attached quotation. In cases involving purchasing goods, many organizations use a certain monetary threshold above which a ''Ringi-sho'' is required. Additionally, ''Ringi-sho'' are used for a wide range of organizational approvals, such as [[personnel changes]], promotions, and contract agreements. The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * [[Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ringi-sho}} [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] [[Category:稟議書]][[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:Approval documents]][[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[ja:稟議書]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]]

In cases involving purchasing goods, many organizations use a certain monetary threshold above which a ''Ringi-sho'' is required. Additionally, ''Ringi-sho'' are used for a wide range of organizational approvals, such as [[personnel changes]], promotions, and contract agreements. The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * [[Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ringi-sho}} [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] [[Category:稟議書]][[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]] [[Category: tier 3]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:Approval documents]][[Category:Approval documents]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:Approval documents]] [[ja:稟議書]] [[Category:New]]

{{Unreferenced|date=August 2020}} [[File:Official document (S39).jpg|thumb|right|250px|A ''Ringi-sho'' (proposal document) created by the Ministry of International Trade and Industry. Many individuals stamped their approval before it reached the final decision-maker, the minister.]] A '''''Ringi-sho''''' (稟議書)According to [[Kōjien]] and [[Daijirin]], the reading of "稟議書" is "Ringi-sho." However, various Japanese dictionaries classify "Ringi" as a [[Hyakushō-yomi|popular reading]] (''hyakushō-yomi'') of "Hingi." is a document created for the purpose of conducting ''[[Ringi]]'' (consultative decision-making). In [[government agencies]], it is often referred to as a '''''Kiansho''''' (起案書), while in corporate settings, alternative terms like '''''Ringi-sho,'' ''Kiansho,'' and ''Ritsuansho''''' may also be used. == Overview == A ''Ringi-sho'' is a document used for conducting ''[[Ringi]]'', a method within organizations such as [[companies]] and government agencies to reduce the time spent convening [[meetings]]. In this method, the person in charge drafts a proposal for a straightforward matter and circulates it among stakeholders to obtain their seals or signatures of approval. In principle, organizational decisions are made through a [[consensus decision-making]] process like meetings. For instance, decisions about executing business operations, such as those made by a [[board of directors]], must be performed by the company's directors themselves, and circular resolutions are only allowed exceptionally. However, meetings consume time and costs, so day-to-day decisions and simpler approvals are often delegated to designated decision-makers. Nonetheless, entrusting all judgment to the final decision-maker could risk neglecting due diligence on individual cases. Furthermore, securing approval from involved parties before making a decision ensures smooth execution post-decision. ''Ringi'' facilitates a broader and more cautious consideration by involving multiple stakeholders before the final decision-maker grants approval. Additionally, documenting the content and process of organizational decision-making in written form is desirable not only for operational convenience but also to make auditing and external review easier. When decisions are made in meetings, meeting minutes or summaries of resolutions are created and recorded. Conversely, when decisions are made via ''Ringi'', the person initiating the proposal prepares a document detailing the proposed decision, which is circulated for stakeholders’ approvals, eventually forming the ''Ringi-sho''. The final decision-maker confirms stakeholders' approvals before authorizing the proposal, completing the organization's decision-making process. This method is particularly suited for decision-making in relatively large organizations. == Format == The format of a ''Ringi-sho'' varies between organizations. It is often an internal document, with each organization establishing its own format. Below is an example: {{Indent| (Title) Regarding the purchase of a company vehicle
(Body) Concerning the above matter, I propose the purchase of a vehicle as outlined below:
Details
# Reason: The delivery vehicle has deteriorated, necessitating a replacement.
# Cost: '''*,000 yen }} Additional details such as the vendor's name, the budget allocated (e.g., ''' yen), actual expenses (e.g., ''' yen), and any differences are typically included, along with an attached quotation. In cases involving purchasing goods, many organizations use a certain monetary threshold above which a ''Ringi-sho'' is required. Additionally, ''Ringi-sho'' are used for a wide range of organizational approvals, such as [[personnel changes]], promotions, and contract agreements. The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * [[Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ringi-sho}} [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] [[Category:稟議書]][[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:Approval documents]][[Category:Approval documents]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}[[Category:Approval documents]] [[ja:稟議書]] [[Category:New]]

Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * [[Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ringi-sho}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}{{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] [[Category:稟議書]] [[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Approval documents]] [[ja:稟議書]]

{{Unreferenced|date=August 2020}} [[File:Official document (S39).jpg|thumb|right|250px|A ''Ringi-sho'' (proposal document) created by the Ministry of International Trade and Industry. Many individuals stamped their approval before it reached the final decision-maker, the minister.]] A '''''Ringi-sho''''' (稟議書)According to [[Kōjien]] and [[Daijirin]], the reading of "稟議書" is "Ringi-sho." However, various Japanese dictionaries classify "Ringi" as a [[Hyakushō-yomi|popular reading]] (''hyakushō-yomi'') of "Hingi." is a document created for the purpose of conducting ''[[Ringi]]'' (consultative decision-making). In [[government agencies]], it is often referred to as a '''''Kiansho''''' (起案書), while in corporate settings, alternative terms like '''''Ringi-sho,'' ''Kiansho,'' and ''Ritsuansho''''' may also be used. == Overview == A ''Ringi-sho'' is a document used for conducting ''[[Ringi]]'', a method within organizations such as [[companies]] and government agencies to reduce the time spent convening [[meetings]]. In this method, the person in charge drafts a proposal for a straightforward matter and circulates it among stakeholders to obtain their seals or signatures of approval. In principle, organizational decisions are made through a [[consensus decision-making]] process like meetings. For instance, decisions about executing business operations, such as those made by a [[board of directors]], must be performed by the company's directors themselves, and circular resolutions are only allowed exceptionally. However, meetings consume time and costs, so day-to-day decisions and simpler approvals are often delegated to designated decision-makers. Nonetheless, entrusting all judgment to the final decision-maker could risk neglecting due diligence on individual cases. Furthermore, securing approval from involved parties before making a decision ensures smooth execution post-decision. ''Ringi'' facilitates a broader and more cautious consideration by involving multiple stakeholders before the final decision-maker grants approval. Additionally, documenting the content and process of organizational decision-making in written form is desirable not only for operational convenience but also to make auditing and external review easier. When decisions are made in meetings, meeting minutes or summaries of resolutions are created and recorded. Conversely, when decisions are made via ''Ringi'', the person initiating the proposal prepares a document detailing the proposed decision, which is circulated for stakeholders’ approvals, eventually forming the ''Ringi-sho''. The final decision-maker confirms stakeholders' approvals before authorizing the proposal, completing the organization's decision-making process. This method is particularly suited for decision-making in relatively large organizations. == Format == The format of a ''Ringi-sho'' varies between organizations. It is often an internal document, with each organization establishing its own format. Below is an example: {{Indent| (Title) Regarding the purchase of a company vehicle
(Body) Concerning the above matter, I propose the purchase of a vehicle as outlined below:
Details
# Reason: The delivery vehicle has deteriorated, necessitating a replacement.
# Cost: '''*,000 yen }} Additional details such as the vendor's name, the budget allocated (e.g., ''' yen), actual expenses (e.g., ''' yen), and any differences are typically included, along with an attached quotation. In cases involving purchasing goods, many organizations use a certain monetary threshold above which a ''Ringi-sho'' is required. Additionally, ''Ringi-sho'' are used for a wide range of organizational approvals, such as [[personnel changes]], promotions, and contract agreements. The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * [[Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] [[Category:稟議書]] [[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Approval documents]] [[ja:稟議書]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ringi-sho}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}

For instance, decisions about executing business operations, such as those made by a [[board of directors]], must be performed by the company's directors themselves, and circular resolutions are only allowed exceptionally. However, meetings consume time and costs, so day-to-day decisions and simpler approvals are often delegated to designated decision-makers. Nonetheless, entrusting all judgment to the final decision-maker could risk neglecting due diligence on individual cases. Furthermore, securing approval from involved parties before making a decision ensures smooth execution post-decision. ''Ringi'' facilitates a broader and more cautious consideration by involving multiple stakeholders before the final decision-maker grants approval. Additionally, documenting the content and process of organizational decision-making in written form is desirable not only for operational convenience but also to make auditing and external review easier. When decisions are made in meetings, meeting minutes or summaries of resolutions are created and recorded. Conversely, when decisions are made via ''Ringi'', the person initiating the proposal prepares a document detailing the proposed decision, which is circulated for stakeholders’ approvals, eventually forming the ''Ringi-sho''. The final decision-maker confirms stakeholders' approvals before authorizing the proposal, completing the organization's decision-making process. This method is particularly suited for decision-making in relatively large organizations. == Format == The format of a ''Ringi-sho'' varies between organizations. It is often an internal document, with each organization establishing its own format. Below is an example: {{Indent| (Title) Regarding the purchase of a company vehicle
(Body) Concerning the above matter, I propose the purchase of a vehicle as outlined below:
Details
# Reason: The delivery vehicle has deteriorated, necessitating a replacement.
# Cost: '''*,000 yen }} Additional details such as the vendor's name, the budget allocated (e.g., ''' yen), actual expenses (e.g., ''' yen), and any differences are typically included, along with an attached quotation. In cases involving purchasing goods, many organizations use a certain monetary threshold above which a ''Ringi-sho'' is required. Additionally, ''Ringi-sho'' are used for a wide range of organizational approvals, such as [[personnel changes]], promotions, and contract agreements. The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * [[Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] [[Category:稟議書]] [[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Approval documents]] [[ja:稟議書]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ringi-sho}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}

This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * [[Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] [[Category:稟議書]] [[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[sn:Approval documents]] [[ja:稟議書]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ringi-sho}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}

{{Unreferenced|date=August 2020}} [[File:Official document (S39).jpg|thumb|right|250px|A ''Ringi-sho'' (proposal document) created by the Ministry of International Trade and Industry. Many individuals stamped their approval before it reached the final decision-maker, the minister.]] A '''''Ringi-sho''''' (稟議書)According to [[Kōjien]] and [[Daijirin]], the reading of "稟議書" is "Ringi-sho." However, various Japanese dictionaries classify "Ringi" as a [[Hyakushō-yomi|popular reading]] (''hyakushō-yomi'') of "Hingi." is a document created for the purpose of conducting ''[[Ringi]]'' (consultative decision-making). In [[government agencies]], it is often referred to as a '''''Kiansho''''' (起案書), while in corporate settings, alternative terms like '''''Ringi-sho,'' ''Kiansho,'' and ''Ritsuansho''''' may also be used. == Overview == A ''Ringi-sho'' is a document used for conducting ''[[Ringi]]'', a method within organizations such as [[companies]] and government agencies to reduce the time spent convening [[meetings]]. In this method, the person in charge drafts a proposal for a straightforward matter and circulates it among stakeholders to obtain their seals or signatures of approval. In principle, organizational decisions are made through a [[consensus decision-making]] process like meetings. For instance, decisions about executing business operations, such as those made by a [[board of directors]], must be performed by the company's directors themselves, and circular resolutions are only allowed exceptionally. However, meetings consume time and costs, so day-to-day decisions and simpler approvals are often delegated to designated decision-makers. Nonetheless, entrusting all judgment to the final decision-maker could risk neglecting due diligence on individual cases. Furthermore, securing approval from involved parties before making a decision ensures smooth execution post-decision. ''Ringi'' facilitates a broader and more cautious consideration by involving multiple stakeholders before the final decision-maker grants approval. Additionally, documenting the content and process of organizational decision-making in written form is desirable not only for operational convenience but also to make auditing and external review easier. When decisions are made in meetings, meeting minutes or summaries of resolutions are created and recorded. Conversely, when decisions are made via ''Ringi'', the person initiating the proposal prepares a document detailing the proposed decision, which is circulated for stakeholders’ approvals, eventually forming the ''Ringi-sho''. The final decision-maker confirms stakeholders' approvals before authorizing the proposal, completing the organization's decision-making process. This method is particularly suited for decision-making in relatively large organizations. == Format == The format of a ''Ringi-sho'' varies between organizations. It is often an internal document, with each organization establishing its own format. Below is an example: {{Indent| (Title) Regarding the purchase of a company vehicle
(Body) Concerning the above matter, I propose the purchase of a vehicle as outlined below:
Details
# Reason: The delivery vehicle has deteriorated, necessitating a replacement.
# Cost: '''*,000 yen }} Additional details such as the vendor's name, the budget allocated (e.g., ''' yen), actual expenses (e.g., ''' yen), and any differences are typically included, along with an attached quotation. In cases involving purchasing goods, many organizations use a certain monetary threshold above which a ''Ringi-sho'' is required. Additionally, ''Ringi-sho'' are used for a wide range of organizational approvals, such as [[personnel changes]], promotions, and contract agreements. The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist}} == See Also == * [[Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] [[Category:稟議書]] [[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Approval documents]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Approval documents]] [[ja:稟議書]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ringi-sho}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents}}

Many individuals stamped their approval before it reached the final decision-maker, the minister.]] A '''''Ringi-sho''''' (稟議書)According to [[Kōjien]] and [[Daijirin]], the reading of "稟議書" is "Ringi-sho." However, various Japanese dictionaries classify "Ringi" as a [[Hyakushō-yomi|popular reading]] (''hyakushō-yomi'') of "Hingi." is a document created for the purpose of conducting ''[[Ringi]]'' (consultative decision-making). In [[government agencies]], it is often referred to as a '''''Kiansho''''' (起案書), while in corporate settings, alternative terms like '''''Ringi-sho,'' ''Kiansho,'' and ''Ritsuansho''''' may also be used. == Overview == A ''Ringi-sho'' is a document used for conducting ''[[Ringi]]'', a method within organizations such as [[companies]] and government agencies to reduce the time spent convening [[meetings]]. In this method, the person in charge drafts a proposal for a straightforward matter and circulates it among stakeholders to obtain their seals or signatures of approval. In principle, organizational decisions are made through a [[consensus decision-making]] process like meetings. For instance, decisions about executing business operations, such as those made by a [[board of directors]], must be performed by the company's directors themselves, and circular resolutions are only allowed exceptionally. However, meetings consume time and costs, so day-to-day decisions and simpler approvals are often delegated to designated decision-makers. Nonetheless, entrusting all judgment to the final decision-maker could risk neglecting due diligence on individual cases. Furthermore, securing approval from involved parties before making a decision ensures smooth execution post-decision. ''Ringi'' facilitates a broader and more cautious consideration by involving multiple stakeholders before the final decision-maker grants approval. Additionally, documenting the content and process of organizational decision-making in written form is desirable not only for operational convenience but also to make auditing and external review easier. When decisions are made in meetings, meeting minutes or summaries of resolutions are created and recorded. Conversely, when decisions are made via ''Ringi'', the person initiating the proposal prepares a document detailing the proposed decision, which is circulated for stakeholders’ approvals, eventually forming the ''Ringi-sho''. The final decision-maker confirms stakeholders' approvals before authorizing the proposal, completing the organization's decision-making process. This method is particularly suited for decision-making in relatively large organizations. == Format == The format of a ''Ringi-sho'' varies between organizations. It is often an internal document, with each organization establishing its own format. Below is an example: {{Indent| (Title) Regarding the purchase of a company vehicle
(Body) Concerning the above matter, I propose the purchase of a vehicle as outlined below:
Details
# Reason: The delivery vehicle has deteriorated, necessitating a replacement.
# Cost: '''*,000 yen |qq=}} Additional details such as the vendor's name, the budget allocated (e.g., ''' yen), actual expenses (e.g., ''' yen), and any differences are typically included, along with an attached quotation. In cases involving purchasing goods, many organizations use a certain monetary threshold above which a ''Ringi-sho'' is required. Additionally, ''Ringi-sho'' are used for a wide range of organizational approvals, such as [[personnel changes]], promotions, and contract agreements. The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == See Also == * [[Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{draft categories| |qq=}} {{draft categories| |qq=}} {{draft categories| |qq=}} {{draft categories| |qq=}} {{draft categories| |qq=}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] [[Category:稟議書]] [[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]] }} [[sn:Approval documents]] [[ja:稟議書]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ringi-sho|qq=}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents|qq=}}
