revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2020年8月}} [[Image:Official document (S39).jpg|thumb|right|250px|通商産業省で作成された稟議書（起案書）。最終的な決裁者である大臣に至るまで、多くの者が承認の印を押している。]] '''稟議書'''（りんぎしょ[[広辞苑]]と[[大辞林]]における「稟議書」の読みは「りんぎしょ」。ただし[[広辞苑]]など各種国語辞典によれば、「稟議」を「りんぎ」と読むのは「ひんぎ」の[[百姓読み|慣用読み]]とされている。）とは、[[稟議]]のために作られる[[文書]]のことである。 なお、[[官庁]]では'''起案書'''ということが多く、企業では稟議書のほか、'''起案書、立案書'''などということもある。 == 概要 == 稟議書は、稟議を行うための文書である。稟議とは、[[会社]]・官庁などの組織において、[[会議]]の開催により消費する時間を減らすため、担当者が簡易案件を作成して関係者に回し、それぞれに同意のための捺印と承認を求めることをいう。 原則として、組織の活動における意思決定は会議による[[合議制]]で行われる。例えば、会社の業務執行の決定などを行う[[取締役会]]は、会社の役員自身が行わなければならず、持ち回り決議は例外的に許されるのみである。しかし、会議は時間と経費を必要とするため、日常的な業務に関する意思決定や簡易な決裁は、決裁権者を定めて一任することも多い。ただ、最終的な決裁権者にすべての判断が求められると、個々の案件についての考慮・審査がおろそかになるおそれがある。また、決裁前に、決定内容に関係する者が承認していれば、決定後の業務執行も円滑に行われる。そこで、決裁権者が決裁する前に、多数の関係者を関与させ、より慎重に幅広く考慮・審査する仕組みが、稟議である。 また、組織において形成された意思の内容および意思の形成過程は、文書の形で記録に残すことが望ましい。これは、業務遂行上の便宜のみならず、後に第三者から見て監査・調査することを容易にするためでもある。会議によって組織の意思決定を行った場合には、会議の結果として決定された事項について、会議録または決定の要点を摘示した文書が作成され、記録に残される。これに対して、稟議によって意思決定する場合には、まず担当者が最終的に決定される意思内容を示した文書を作成し、この文書を関係者が回覧して、承認したことを示すサイン・[[印章|印]]を記した稟議書を作成する。最終的な決裁権者は、関係者が決定内容を承認していることを確認して決裁し、組織としての最終的な意思決定が行われる。 このように、稟議および稟議書は、比較的大規模な組織の意思決定に用いられる方法である。 == 書式 == 稟議書の書式は、組織により異なる。各組織の内部文書とされることが多いため、書式は各組織ごとに、独自に定められている。下記に一例を挙げる。 {{Indent| （題名）業務用自動車の購入について
（本文）標記の件、下記の通り自動車を購入したくお伺いいたします。
記
#理由　　配送用自動車が老朽化しており、買い替えの必要が生じたため。
#価格　　＊＊＊円 }} 以下、購入先の業者名、[[予算]]は＊＊円計上してあったが、実績（実際の額）は＊＊円で差額が＊＊円、といった内容を記載し、[[見積書]]を添付する。 また、上記のような物品購入の場合、ある一定の基準額があり、それを超える場合に稟議書を書く企業が多いようである。 また、[[人事異動]]・昇進、契約の締結など、組織の決裁を仰ぐあらゆる事柄に稟議書が使われる。 まず、稟議を起案する本人、そしてその上席の上司、最終的には決裁権を持つ者の印鑑（またはサイン）をもらうことによって決裁とするのである。決裁権者は概ね金額によって決まる。例えば＊＊万円以上の契約の場合は社長の決裁が必要、といった具合に社内で基準をつくっている。 == 稟議書の保管・管理== 稟議および稟議書は企業や組織の内部文書として、[[総務部]]等の部署が保管・管理する事が多い。 日本では一般的な稟議書については法律で文書保存期間は定められていないため、各組織は内部規程等で自ら保存期間を決定している。 == 稟議書の監査 == [[内部統制]]の評価手続や業務監査において、稟議書が監査対象となることがある。決裁権限規定に照らして適切な決裁権限者による決裁が行われているか、稟議内容が適切に文書化されているか、適切に整理・保管されているかなどが検討される。 また、[[会計監査]]や[[税務調査]]においても、会計処理や税務処理の前提となる事実（意思決定の経緯、取引の目的・内容など）を把握するため稟議書が閲覧されることもある。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[内部統制]] * [[総務]] {{DEFAULTSORT:りんきしよ}} [[Category:企業統治]] [[Category:日本の行政]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[稟議書]] to [[Approval documents]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2020年8月}} [[Image:Official document (S39).jpg|thumb|right|250px|通商産業省で作成された稟議書（起案書）。最終的な決裁者である大臣に至るまで、多くの者が承認の印を押している。]] '''稟議書'''（りんぎしょ[[広辞苑]]と[[大辞林]]における「稟議書」の読みは「りんぎしょ」。ただし[[広辞苑]]など各種国語辞典によれば、「稟議」を「りんぎ」と読むのは「ひんぎ」の[[百姓読み|慣用読み]]とされている。）とは、[[稟議]]のために作られる[[文書]]のことである。 なお、[[官庁]]では'''起案書'''ということが多く、企業では稟議書のほか、'''起案書、立案書'''などということもある。 == 概要 == 稟議書は、稟議を行うための文書である。稟議とは、[[会社]]・官庁などの組織において、[[会議]]の開催により消費する時間を減らすため、担当者が簡易案件を作成して関係者に回し、それぞれに同意のための捺印と承認を求めることをいう。 原則として、組織の活動における意思決定は会議による[[合議制]]で行われる。例えば、会社の業務執行の決定などを行う[[取締役会]]は、会社の役員自身が行わなければならず、持ち回り決議は例外的に許されるのみである。しかし、会議は時間と経費を必要とするため、日常的な業務に関する意思決定や簡易な決裁は、決裁権者を定めて一任することも多い。ただ、最終的な決裁権者にすべての判断が求められると、個々の案件についての考慮・審査がおろそかになるおそれがある。また、決裁前に、決定内容に関係する者が承認していれば、決定後の業務執行も円滑に行われる。そこで、決裁権者が決裁する前に、多数の関係者を関与させ、より慎重に幅広く考慮・審査する仕組みが、稟議である。 また、組織において形成された意思の内容および意思の形成過程は、文書の形で記録に残すことが望ましい。これは、業務遂行上の便宜のみならず、後に第三者から見て監査・調査することを容易にするためでもある。会議によって組織の意思決定を行った場合には、会議の結果として決定された事項について、会議録または決定の要点を摘示した文書が作成され、記録に残される。これに対して、稟議によって意思決定する場合には、まず担当者が最終的に決定される意思内容を示した文書を作成し、この文書を関係者が回覧して、承認したことを示すサイン・[[印章|印]]を記した稟議書を作成する。最終的な決裁権者は、関係者が決定内容を承認していることを確認して決裁し、組織としての最終的な意思決定が行われる。 このように、稟議および稟議書は、比較的大規模な組織の意思決定に用いられる方法である。 == 書式 == 稟議書の書式は、組織により異なる。各組織の内部文書とされることが多いため、書式は各組織ごとに、独自に定められている。下記に一例を挙げる。 {{Indent| （題名）業務用自動車の購入について
（本文）標記の件、下記の通り自動車を購入したくお伺いいたします。
記
#理由　　配送用自動車が老朽化しており、買い替えの必要が生じたため。
#価格　　＊＊＊円 }} 以下、購入先の業者名、[[予算]]は＊＊円計上してあったが、実績（実際の額）は＊＊円で差額が＊＊円、といった内容を記載し、[[見積書]]を添付する。 また、上記のような物品購入の場合、ある一定の基準額があり、それを超える場合に稟議書を書く企業が多いようである。 また、[[人事異動]]・昇進、契約の締結など、組織の決裁を仰ぐあらゆる事柄に稟議書が使われる。 まず、稟議を起案する本人、そしてその上席の上司、最終的には決裁権を持つ者の印鑑（またはサイン）をもらうことによって決裁とするのである。決裁権者は概ね金額によって決まる。例えば＊＊万円以上の契約の場合は社長の決裁が必要、といった具合に社内で基準をつくっている。 == 稟議書の保管・管理== 稟議および稟議書は企業や組織の内部文書として、[[総務部]]等の部署が保管・管理する事が多い。 日本では一般的な稟議書については法律で文書保存期間は定められていないため、各組織は内部規程等で自ら保存期間を決定している。 == 稟議書の監査 == [[内部統制]]の評価手続や業務監査において、稟議書が監査対象となることがある。決裁権限規定に照らして適切な決裁権限者による決裁が行われているか、稟議内容が適切に文書化されているか、適切に整理・保管されているかなどが検討される。 また、[[会計監査]]や[[税務調査]]においても、会計処理や税務処理の前提となる事実（意思決定の経緯、取引の目的・内容など）を把握するため稟議書が閲覧されることもある。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[内部統制]] * [[総務]] {{DEFAULTSORT:りんきしよ}} [[Category:企業統治]] [[Category:日本の行政]]

{{Unreferenced|date=August 2020|qq=}} [[File:Official document (S39).jpg|thumb|right|250px|A ''Ringi-sho'' (proposal document) created by the Ministry of International Trade and Industry. Many individuals stamped their approval before it reached the final decision-maker, the minister.]] A '''''Ringi-sho''''' (稟議書)According to [[Kōjien]] and [[Daijirin]], the reading of "稟議書" is "Ringi-sho." However, various Japanese dictionaries classify "Ringi" as a [[Hyakushō-yomi|popular reading]] (''hyakushō-yomi'') of "Hingi." is a document created for the purpose of conducting ''[[Ringi]]'' (consultative decision-making). In [[government agencies]], it is often referred to as a '''''Kiansho''''' (起案書), while in corporate settings, alternative terms like '''''Ringi-sho,'' ''Kiansho,'' and ''Ritsuansho''''' may also be used. == Overview == A ''Ringi-sho'' is a document used for conducting ''[[Ringi]]'', a method within organizations such as [[companies]] and government agencies to reduce the time spent convening [[meetings]]. In this method, the person in charge drafts a proposal for a straightforward matter and circulates it among stakeholders to obtain their seals or signatures of approval. In principle, organizational decisions are made through a [[consensus decision-making]] process like meetings. For instance, decisions about executing business operations, such as those made by a [[board of directors]], must be performed by the company's directors themselves, and circular resolutions are only allowed exceptionally. However, meetings consume time and costs, so day-to-day decisions and simpler approvals are often delegated to designated decision-makers. Nonetheless, entrusting all judgment to the final decision-maker could risk neglecting due diligence on individual cases. Furthermore, securing approval from involved parties before making a decision ensures smooth execution post-decision. ''Ringi'' facilitates a broader and more cautious consideration by involving multiple stakeholders before the final decision-maker grants approval. Additionally, documenting the content and process of organizational decision-making in written form is desirable not only for operational convenience but also to make auditing and external review easier. When decisions are made in meetings, meeting minutes or summaries of resolutions are created and recorded. Conversely, when decisions are made via ''Ringi'', the person initiating the proposal prepares a document detailing the proposed decision, which is circulated for stakeholders’ approvals, eventually forming the ''Ringi-sho''. The final decision-maker confirms stakeholders' approvals before authorizing the proposal, completing the organization's decision-making process. This method is particularly suited for decision-making in relatively large organizations. == Format == The format of a ''Ringi-sho'' varies between organizations. It is often an internal document, with each organization establishing its own format. Below is an example: {{Indent| (Title) Regarding the purchase of a company vehicle
(Body) Concerning the above matter, I propose the purchase of a vehicle as outlined below:
Details
# Reason: The delivery vehicle has deteriorated, necessitating a replacement.
# Cost: '''*,000 yen }} Additional details such as the vendor's name, the budget allocated (e.g., ''' yen), actual expenses (e.g., ''' yen), and any differences are typically included, along with an attached quotation. In cases involving purchasing goods, many organizations use a certain monetary threshold above which a ''Ringi-sho'' is required. Additionally, ''Ringi-sho'' are used for a wide range of organizational approvals, such as [[personnel changes]], promotions, and contract agreements. The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == See Also == * [[Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{translated page|ja|稟議書|version=96919592|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Ringi-sho|qq=}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Approval documents|qq=}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] [[Category:稟議書]] [[Category:Approval documents]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]] }} [[sn:Approval documents]] [[ja:稟議書]]

The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == See Also == * [[Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{translated page|ja|稟議書|version=96919592|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] [[Category:稟議書]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[sn:Approval documents]] [[ja:稟議書]]

{{Unreferenced|date=August 2020|qq=}} [[File:Official document (S39).jpg|thumb|right|250px|A ''Ringi-sho'' (proposal document) created by the Ministry of International Trade and Industry. Many individuals stamped their approval before it reached the final decision-maker, the minister.]] A '''''Ringi-sho''''' (稟議書)According to [[Kōjien]] and [[Daijirin]], the reading of "稟議書" is "Ringi-sho." However, various Japanese dictionaries classify "Ringi" as a [[Hyakushō-yomi|popular reading]] (''hyakushō-yomi'') of "Hingi." is a document created for the purpose of conducting ''[[Ringi]]'' (consultative decision-making). In [[government agencies]], it is often referred to as a '''''Kiansho''''' (起案書), while in corporate settings, alternative terms like '''''Ringi-sho,'' ''Kiansho,'' and ''Ritsuansho''''' may also be used. == Overview == A ''Ringi-sho'' is a document used for conducting ''[[Ringi]]'', a method within organizations such as [[companies]] and government agencies to reduce the time spent convening [[meetings]]. In this method, the person in charge drafts a proposal for a straightforward matter and circulates it among stakeholders to obtain their seals or signatures of approval. In principle, organizational decisions are made through a [[consensus decision-making]] process like meetings. For instance, decisions about executing business operations, such as those made by a [[board of directors]], must be performed by the company's directors themselves, and circular resolutions are only allowed exceptionally. However, meetings consume time and costs, so day-to-day decisions and simpler approvals are often delegated to designated decision-makers. Nonetheless, entrusting all judgment to the final decision-maker could risk neglecting due diligence on individual cases. Furthermore, securing approval from involved parties before making a decision ensures smooth execution post-decision. ''Ringi'' facilitates a broader and more cautious consideration by involving multiple stakeholders before the final decision-maker grants approval. Additionally, documenting the content and process of organizational decision-making in written form is desirable not only for operational convenience but also to make auditing and external review easier. When decisions are made in meetings, meeting minutes or summaries of resolutions are created and recorded. Conversely, when decisions are made via ''Ringi'', the person initiating the proposal prepares a document detailing the proposed decision, which is circulated for stakeholders’ approvals, eventually forming the ''Ringi-sho''. The final decision-maker confirms stakeholders' approvals before authorizing the proposal, completing the organization's decision-making process. This method is particularly suited for decision-making in relatively large organizations. == Format == The format of a ''Ringi-sho'' varies between organizations. It is often an internal document, with each organization establishing its own format. Below is an example: {{Indent| (Title) Regarding the purchase of a company vehicle
(Body) Concerning the above matter, I propose the purchase of a vehicle as outlined below:
Details
# Reason: The delivery vehicle has deteriorated, necessitating a replacement.
# Cost: '''*,000 yen }} Additional details such as the vendor's name, the budget allocated (e.g., ''' yen), actual expenses (e.g., ''' yen), and any differences are typically included, along with an attached quotation. In cases involving purchasing goods, many organizations use a certain monetary threshold above which a ''Ringi-sho'' is required. Additionally, ''Ringi-sho'' are used for a wide range of organizational approvals, such as [[personnel changes]], promotions, and contract agreements. The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == See Also == * [[Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{translated page|ja|稟議書|version=96919592|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[sn:Approval documents]] [[ja:稟議書]]

For instance, decisions about executing business operations, such as those made by a [[board of directors]], must be performed by the company's directors themselves, and circular resolutions are only allowed exceptionally. However, meetings consume time and costs, so day-to-day decisions and simpler approvals are often delegated to designated decision-makers. Nonetheless, entrusting all judgment to the final decision-maker could risk neglecting due diligence on individual cases. Furthermore, securing approval from involved parties before making a decision ensures smooth execution post-decision. ''Ringi'' facilitates a broader and more cautious consideration by involving multiple stakeholders before the final decision-maker grants approval. Additionally, documenting the content and process of organizational decision-making in written form is desirable not only for operational convenience but also to make auditing and external review easier. When decisions are made in meetings, meeting minutes or summaries of resolutions are created and recorded. Conversely, when decisions are made via ''Ringi'', the person initiating the proposal prepares a document detailing the proposed decision, which is circulated for stakeholders’ approvals, eventually forming the ''Ringi-sho''. The final decision-maker confirms stakeholders' approvals before authorizing the proposal, completing the organization's decision-making process. This method is particularly suited for decision-making in relatively large organizations. == Format == The format of a ''Ringi-sho'' varies between organizations. It is often an internal document, with each organization establishing its own format. Below is an example: {{Indent| (Title) Regarding the purchase of a company vehicle
(Body) Concerning the above matter, I propose the purchase of a vehicle as outlined below:
Details
# Reason: The delivery vehicle has deteriorated, necessitating a replacement.
# Cost: '''*,000 yen }} Additional details such as the vendor's name, the budget allocated (e.g., ''' yen), actual expenses (e.g., ''' yen), and any differences are typically included, along with an attached quotation. In cases involving purchasing goods, many organizations use a certain monetary threshold above which a ''Ringi-sho'' is required. Additionally, ''Ringi-sho'' are used for a wide range of organizational approvals, such as [[personnel changes]], promotions, and contract agreements. The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == See Also == * [[Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{translated page|ja|稟議書|version=96919592|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[sn:Approval documents]] [[ja:稟議書]] [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]]

The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[:en:Internal controls|Internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == See Also == * [[:en:Internal controls|Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{translated page|ja|稟議書|version=96919592|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[sn:Approval documents]] [[ja:稟議書]] [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]]

Many individuals stamped their approval before it reached the final decision-maker, the minister.]] A '''''Ringi-sho''''' (稟議書)According to [[Kōjien]] and [[Daijirin]], the reading of "稟議書" is "Ringi-sho." However, various Japanese dictionaries classify "Ringi" as a [[Hyakushō-yomi|popular reading]] (''hyakushō-yomi'') of "Hingi." is a document created for the purpose of conducting ''[[:en:Ringi|Ringi]]'' (consultative decision-making). In [[:en:government agencies|government agencies]], it is often referred to as a '''''Kiansho''''' (起案書), while in corporate settings, alternative terms like '''''Ringi-sho,'' ''Kiansho,'' and ''Ritsuansho''''' may also be used. == Overview == A ''Ringi-sho'' is a document used for conducting ''[[:en:Ringi|Ringi]]'', a method within organizations such as [[:en:companies|companies]] and government agencies to reduce the time spent convening [[:en:meetings|meetings]]. In this method, the person in charge drafts a proposal for a straightforward matter and circulates it among stakeholders to obtain their seals or signatures of approval. In principle, organizational decisions are made through a [[:en:consensus decision-making|consensus decision-making]] process like meetings. For instance, decisions about executing business operations, such as those made by a [[:en:board of directors|board of directors]], must be performed by the company's directors themselves, and circular resolutions are only allowed exceptionally. However, meetings consume time and costs, so day-to-day decisions and simpler approvals are often delegated to designated decision-makers. Nonetheless, entrusting all judgment to the final decision-maker could risk neglecting due diligence on individual cases. Furthermore, securing approval from involved parties before making a decision ensures smooth execution post-decision. ''Ringi'' facilitates a broader and more cautious consideration by involving multiple stakeholders before the final decision-maker grants approval. Additionally, documenting the content and process of organizational decision-making in written form is desirable not only for operational convenience but also to make auditing and external review easier. When decisions are made in meetings, meeting minutes or summaries of resolutions are created and recorded. Conversely, when decisions are made via ''Ringi'', the person initiating the proposal prepares a document detailing the proposed decision, which is circulated for stakeholders’ approvals, eventually forming the ''Ringi-sho''. The final decision-maker confirms stakeholders' approvals before authorizing the proposal, completing the organization's decision-making process. This method is particularly suited for decision-making in relatively large organizations. == Format == The format of a ''Ringi-sho'' varies between organizations. It is often an internal document, with each organization establishing its own format. Below is an example: {{Indent| (Title) Regarding the purchase of a company vehicle
(Body) Concerning the above matter, I propose the purchase of a vehicle as outlined below:
Details
# Reason: The delivery vehicle has deteriorated, necessitating a replacement.
# Cost: '''*,000 yen }} Additional details such as the vendor's name, the budget allocated (e.g., ''' yen), actual expenses (e.g., ''' yen), and any differences are typically included, along with an attached quotation. In cases involving purchasing goods, many organizations use a certain monetary threshold above which a ''Ringi-sho'' is required. Additionally, ''Ringi-sho'' are used for a wide range of organizational approvals, such as [[personnel changes]], promotions, and contract agreements. The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[:en:Internal controls|Internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == See Also == * [[:en:Internal controls|Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{translated page|ja|稟議書|version=96919592|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[sn:Approval documents]] [[ja:稟議書]] [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]]

The final decision-maker confirms stakeholders' approvals before authorizing the proposal, completing the organization's decision-making process. This method is particularly suited for decision-making in relatively large organizations. == Format == The format of a ''Ringi-sho'' varies between organizations. It is often an internal document, with each organization establishing its own format. Below is an example: {{Indent| (Title) Regarding the purchase of a company vehicle
(Body) Concerning the above matter, I propose the purchase of a vehicle as outlined below:
Details
# Reason: The delivery vehicle has deteriorated, necessitating a replacement.
# Cost: '''*,000 yen }} Additional details such as the vendor's name, the budget allocated (e.g., ''' yen), actual expenses (e.g., ''' yen), and any differences are typically included, along with an attached quotation. In cases involving purchasing goods, many organizations use a certain monetary threshold above which a ''Ringi-sho'' is required. Additionally, ''Ringi-sho'' are used for a wide range of organizational approvals, such as [[personnel changes]], promotions, and contract agreements. The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[:en:Internal controls|Internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == See Also == * [[:en:Internal controls|Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{translated page|ja|稟議書|version=96919592|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[sn:Approval documents]] [[ja:稟議書]] [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] {{wikidata link|Q11596356}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11596356]] Wikidata name: ringisho [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11596356]] Wikidata name: ringisho [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=August 2020|qq=}} [[File:Official document (S39).jpg|thumb|right|250px|A ''Ringi-sho'' (proposal document) created by the Ministry of International Trade and Industry. Many individuals stamped their approval before it reached the final decision-maker, the minister.]] A '''''Ringi-sho''''' (稟議書)According to [[Kōjien]] and [[Daijirin]], the reading of "稟議書" is "Ringi-sho." However, various Japanese dictionaries classify "Ringi" as a [[Hyakushō-yomi|popular reading]] (''hyakushō-yomi'') of "Hingi." is a document created for the purpose of conducting ''[[:en:Ringi|Ringi]]'' (consultative decision-making). In [[:en:government agencies|government agencies]], it is often referred to as a '''''Kiansho''''' (起案書), while in corporate settings, alternative terms like '''''Ringi-sho,'' ''Kiansho,'' and ''Ritsuansho''''' may also be used. == Overview == A ''Ringi-sho'' is a document used for conducting ''[[:en:Ringi|Ringi]]'', a method within organizations such as [[:en:companies|companies]] and government agencies to reduce the time spent convening [[:en:meetings|meetings]]. In this method, the person in charge drafts a proposal for a straightforward matter and circulates it among stakeholders to obtain their seals or signatures of approval. In principle, organizational decisions are made through a [[:en:consensus decision-making|consensus decision-making]] process like meetings. For instance, decisions about executing business operations, such as those made by a [[:en:board of directors|board of directors]], must be performed by the company's directors themselves, and circular resolutions are only allowed exceptionally. However, meetings consume time and costs, so day-to-day decisions and simpler approvals are often delegated to designated decision-makers. Nonetheless, entrusting all judgment to the final decision-maker could risk neglecting due diligence on individual cases. Furthermore, securing approval from involved parties before making a decision ensures smooth execution post-decision. ''Ringi'' facilitates a broader and more cautious consideration by involving multiple stakeholders before the final decision-maker grants approval. Additionally, documenting the content and process of organizational decision-making in written form is desirable not only for operational convenience but also to make auditing and external review easier. When decisions are made in meetings, meeting minutes or summaries of resolutions are created and recorded. Conversely, when decisions are made via ''Ringi'', the person initiating the proposal prepares a document detailing the proposed decision, which is circulated for stakeholders’ approvals, eventually forming the ''Ringi-sho''. The final decision-maker confirms stakeholders' approvals before authorizing the proposal, completing the organization's decision-making process. This method is particularly suited for decision-making in relatively large organizations. == Format == The format of a ''Ringi-sho'' varies between organizations. It is often an internal document, with each organization establishing its own format. Below is an example: {{Indent| (Title) Regarding the purchase of a company vehicle
(Body) Concerning the above matter, I propose the purchase of a vehicle as outlined below:
Details
# Reason: The delivery vehicle has deteriorated, necessitating a replacement.
# Cost: '''*,000 yen }} Additional details such as the vendor's name, the budget allocated (e.g., ''' yen), actual expenses (e.g., ''' yen), and any differences are typically included, along with an attached quotation. In cases involving purchasing goods, many organizations use a certain monetary threshold above which a ''Ringi-sho'' is required. Additionally, ''Ringi-sho'' are used for a wide range of organizational approvals, such as [[personnel changes]], promotions, and contract agreements. The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[:en:Internal controls|Internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == See Also == * [[:en:Internal controls|Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{translated page|ja|稟議書|version=96919592|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[ja:稟議書]] [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] {{wikidata link|Q11596356}}

Many individuals stamped their approval before it reached the final decision-maker, the minister.]] A '''''Ringi-sho''''' (稟議書)According to [[Kōjien]] and [[Daijirin]], the reading of "稟議書" is "Ringi-sho." However, various Japanese dictionaries classify "Ringi" as a [[Hyakushō-yomi|popular reading]] (''hyakushō-yomi'') of "Hingi." is a document created for the purpose of conducting ''[[:en:Ringi|Ringi]]'' (consultative decision-making). In [[:en:government agencies|government agencies]], it is often referred to as a '''''Kiansho''''' (起案書), while in corporate settings, alternative terms like '''''Ringi-sho,'' ''Kiansho,'' and ''Ritsuansho''''' may also be used. == Overview == A ''Ringi-sho'' is a document used for conducting ''[[:en:Ringi|Ringi]]'', a method within organizations such as [[:en:companies|companies]] and government agencies to reduce the time spent convening [[:en:meetings|meetings]]. In this method, the person in charge drafts a proposal for a straightforward matter and circulates it among stakeholders to obtain their seals or signatures of approval. In principle, organizational decisions are made through a [[:en:consensus decision-making|consensus decision-making]] process like meetings. For instance, decisions about executing business operations, such as those made by a [[:en:board of directors|board of directors]], must be performed by the company's directors themselves, and circular resolutions are only allowed exceptionally. However, meetings consume time and costs, so day-to-day decisions and simpler approvals are often delegated to designated decision-makers. Nonetheless, entrusting all judgment to the final decision-maker could risk neglecting due diligence on individual cases. Furthermore, securing approval from involved parties before making a decision ensures smooth execution post-decision. ''Ringi'' facilitates a broader and more cautious consideration by involving multiple stakeholders before the final decision-maker grants approval. Additionally, documenting the content and process of organizational decision-making in written form is desirable not only for operational convenience but also to make auditing and external review easier. When decisions are made in meetings, meeting minutes or summaries of resolutions are created and recorded. Conversely, when decisions are made via ''Ringi'', the person initiating the proposal prepares a document detailing the proposed decision, which is circulated for stakeholders’ approvals, eventually forming the ''Ringi-sho''. The final decision-maker confirms stakeholders' approvals before authorizing the proposal, completing the organization's decision-making process. This method is particularly suited for decision-making in relatively large organizations. == Format == The format of a ''Ringi-sho'' varies between organizations. It is often an internal document, with each organization establishing its own format. Below is an example: {{Indent| (Title) Regarding the purchase of a company vehicle
(Body) Concerning the above matter, I propose the purchase of a vehicle as outlined below:
Details
# Reason: The delivery vehicle has deteriorated, necessitating a replacement.
# Cost: '''*,000 yen }} Additional details such as the vendor's name, the budget allocated (e.g., ''' yen), actual expenses (e.g., ''' yen), and any differences are typically included, along with an attached quotation. In cases involving purchasing goods, many organizations use a certain monetary threshold above which a ''Ringi-sho'' is required. Additionally, ''Ringi-sho'' are used for a wide range of organizational approvals, such as [[personnel changes]], promotions, and contract agreements. The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[:en:Internal controls|Internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == See Also == * [[:en:Internal controls|Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{translated page|ja|稟議書|version=96919592|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[ja:稟議書]] [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] {{wikidata link|Q11596356}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

The final decision-maker confirms stakeholders' approvals before authorizing the proposal, completing the organization's decision-making process. This method is particularly suited for decision-making in relatively large organizations. == Format == The format of a ''Ringi-sho'' varies between organizations. It is often an internal document, with each organization establishing its own format. Below is an example: {{Indent| (Title) Regarding the purchase of a company vehicle
(Body) Concerning the above matter, I propose the purchase of a vehicle as outlined below:
Details
# Reason: The delivery vehicle has deteriorated, necessitating a replacement.
# Cost: '''*,000 yen }} Additional details such as the vendor's name, the budget allocated (e.g., ''' yen), actual expenses (e.g., ''' yen), and any differences are typically included, along with an attached quotation. In cases involving purchasing goods, many organizations use a certain monetary threshold above which a ''Ringi-sho'' is required. Additionally, ''Ringi-sho'' are used for a wide range of organizational approvals, such as [[personnel changes]], promotions, and contract agreements. The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[:en:Internal controls|Internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == See Also == * [[:en:Internal controls|Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{translated page|ja|稟議書|version=96919592|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[ja:稟議書]] [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] {{wikidata link|Q11596356}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

[[ja:稟議書]] [[ja:稟議書]] [[ja:稟議書]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11596356]] Wikidata name: ringisho [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11596356]] Wikidata name: ringisho [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=August 2020|qq=}} [[File:Official document (S39).jpg|thumb|right|250px|A ''Ringi-sho'' (proposal document) created by the Ministry of International Trade and Industry. Many individuals stamped their approval before it reached the final decision-maker, the minister.]] A '''''Ringi-sho''''' (稟議書)According to [[Kōjien]] and [[Daijirin]], the reading of "稟議書" is "Ringi-sho." However, various Japanese dictionaries classify "Ringi" as a [[Hyakushō-yomi|popular reading]] (''hyakushō-yomi'') of "Hingi." is a document created for the purpose of conducting ''[[:en:Ringi|Ringi]]'' (consultative decision-making). In [[:en:government agencies|government agencies]], it is often referred to as a '''''Kiansho''''' (起案書), while in corporate settings, alternative terms like '''''Ringi-sho,'' ''Kiansho,'' and ''Ritsuansho''''' may also be used. == Overview == A ''Ringi-sho'' is a document used for conducting ''[[:en:Ringi|Ringi]]'', a method within organizations such as [[:en:companies|companies]] and government agencies to reduce the time spent convening [[:en:meetings|meetings]]. In this method, the person in charge drafts a proposal for a straightforward matter and circulates it among stakeholders to obtain their seals or signatures of approval. In principle, organizational decisions are made through a [[:en:consensus decision-making|consensus decision-making]] process like meetings. For instance, decisions about executing business operations, such as those made by a [[:en:board of directors|board of directors]], must be performed by the company's directors themselves, and circular resolutions are only allowed exceptionally. However, meetings consume time and costs, so day-to-day decisions and simpler approvals are often delegated to designated decision-makers. Nonetheless, entrusting all judgment to the final decision-maker could risk neglecting due diligence on individual cases. Furthermore, securing approval from involved parties before making a decision ensures smooth execution post-decision. ''Ringi'' facilitates a broader and more cautious consideration by involving multiple stakeholders before the final decision-maker grants approval. Additionally, documenting the content and process of organizational decision-making in written form is desirable not only for operational convenience but also to make auditing and external review easier. When decisions are made in meetings, meeting minutes or summaries of resolutions are created and recorded. Conversely, when decisions are made via ''Ringi'', the person initiating the proposal prepares a document detailing the proposed decision, which is circulated for stakeholders’ approvals, eventually forming the ''Ringi-sho''. The final decision-maker confirms stakeholders' approvals before authorizing the proposal, completing the organization's decision-making process. This method is particularly suited for decision-making in relatively large organizations. == Format == The format of a ''Ringi-sho'' varies between organizations. It is often an internal document, with each organization establishing its own format. Below is an example: {{Indent| (Title) Regarding the purchase of a company vehicle
(Body) Concerning the above matter, I propose the purchase of a vehicle as outlined below:
Details
# Reason: The delivery vehicle has deteriorated, necessitating a replacement.
# Cost: '''*,000 yen }} Additional details such as the vendor's name, the budget allocated (e.g., ''' yen), actual expenses (e.g., ''' yen), and any differences are typically included, along with an attached quotation. In cases involving purchasing goods, many organizations use a certain monetary threshold above which a ''Ringi-sho'' is required. Additionally, ''Ringi-sho'' are used for a wide range of organizational approvals, such as [[personnel changes]], promotions, and contract agreements. The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[:en:Internal controls|Internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == See Also == * [[:en:Internal controls|Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{translated page|ja|稟議書|version=96919592|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] }} [[ja:稟議書]] [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] {{wikidata link|Q11596356}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

[[ja:稟議書]] [[ja:稟議書]] [[ja:稟議書]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11596356]] Wikidata name: ringisho [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11596356]] Wikidata name: ringisho [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=August 2020|qq=}} [[File:Official document (S39).jpg|thumb|right|250px|A ''Ringi-sho'' (proposal document) created by the Ministry of International Trade and Industry. Many individuals stamped their approval before it reached the final decision-maker, the minister.]] A '''''Ringi-sho''''' (稟議書)According to [[Kōjien]] and [[Daijirin]], the reading of "稟議書" is "Ringi-sho." However, various Japanese dictionaries classify "Ringi" as a [[Hyakushō-yomi|popular reading]] (''hyakushō-yomi'') of "Hingi." is a document created for the purpose of conducting ''[[:en:Ringi|Ringi]]'' (consultative decision-making). In [[:en:government agencies|government agencies]], it is often referred to as a '''''Kiansho''''' (起案書), while in corporate settings, alternative terms like '''''Ringi-sho,'' ''Kiansho,'' and ''Ritsuansho''''' may also be used. == Overview == A ''Ringi-sho'' is a document used for conducting ''[[:en:Ringi|Ringi]]'', a method within organizations such as [[:en:companies|companies]] and government agencies to reduce the time spent convening [[:en:meetings|meetings]]. In this method, the person in charge drafts a proposal for a straightforward matter and circulates it among stakeholders to obtain their seals or signatures of approval. In principle, organizational decisions are made through a {{ill|consensus decision-making|en|consensus decision-making|WD=Q188577|lt=consensus decision-making}} process like meetings. For instance, decisions about executing business operations, such as those made by a {{ill|board of directors|en|board of directors|WD=Q188628|lt=board of directors}}, must be performed by the company's directors themselves, and circular resolutions are only allowed exceptionally. However, meetings consume time and costs, so day-to-day decisions and simpler approvals are often delegated to designated decision-makers. Nonetheless, entrusting all judgment to the final decision-maker could risk neglecting due diligence on individual cases. Furthermore, securing approval from involved parties before making a decision ensures smooth execution post-decision. ''Ringi'' facilitates a broader and more cautious consideration by involving multiple stakeholders before the final decision-maker grants approval. Additionally, documenting the content and process of organizational decision-making in written form is desirable not only for operational convenience but also to make auditing and external review easier. When decisions are made in meetings, meeting minutes or summaries of resolutions are created and recorded. Conversely, when decisions are made via ''Ringi'', the person initiating the proposal prepares a document detailing the proposed decision, which is circulated for stakeholders’ approvals, eventually forming the ''Ringi-sho''. The final decision-maker confirms stakeholders' approvals before authorizing the proposal, completing the organization's decision-making process. This method is particularly suited for decision-making in relatively large organizations. == Format == The format of a ''Ringi-sho'' varies between organizations. It is often an internal document, with each organization establishing its own format. Below is an example: {{Indent| (Title) Regarding the purchase of a company vehicle
(Body) Concerning the above matter, I propose the purchase of a vehicle as outlined below:
Details
# Reason: The delivery vehicle has deteriorated, necessitating a replacement.
# Cost: '''*,000 yen }} Additional details such as the vendor's name, the budget allocated (e.g., ''' yen), actual expenses (e.g., ''' yen), and any differences are typically included, along with an attached quotation. In cases involving purchasing goods, many organizations use a certain monetary threshold above which a ''Ringi-sho'' is required. Additionally, ''Ringi-sho'' are used for a wide range of organizational approvals, such as [[personnel changes]], promotions, and contract agreements. The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[:en:Internal controls|Internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == See Also == * [[:en:Internal controls|Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{translated page|ja|稟議書|version=96919592|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:稟議書]] {{wikidata link|Q11596356}} [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

The final decision-maker confirms stakeholders' approvals before authorizing the proposal, completing the organization's decision-making process. This method is particularly suited for decision-making in relatively large organizations. == Format == The format of a ''Ringi-sho'' varies between organizations. It is often an internal document, with each organization establishing its own format. Below is an example: {{Indent| (Title) Regarding the purchase of a company vehicle
(Body) Concerning the above matter, I propose the purchase of a vehicle as outlined below:
Details
# Reason: The delivery vehicle has deteriorated, necessitating a replacement.
# Cost: '''*,000 yen }} Additional details such as the vendor's name, the budget allocated (e.g., ''' yen), actual expenses (e.g., ''' yen), and any differences are typically included, along with an attached quotation. In cases involving purchasing goods, many organizations use a certain monetary threshold above which a ''Ringi-sho'' is required. Additionally, ''Ringi-sho'' are used for a wide range of organizational approvals, such as [[personnel changes]], promotions, and contract agreements. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[:en:Internal controls|Internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == See Also == * [[:en:Internal controls|Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{translated page|ja|稟議書|version=96919592|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:稟議書]] {{wikidata link|Q11596356}} [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[:en:Internal controls|Internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == See Also == * [[:en:Internal controls|Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{translated page|ja|稟議書|version=96919592|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:稟議書]] {{wikidata link|Q11596356}} [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages]]

[[ja:稟議書]] [[ja:稟議書]] [[ja:稟議書]] {{Unreferenced|date=August 2020|qq=}} [[File:Official document (S39).jpg|thumb|right|250px|A ''Ringi-sho'' (proposal document) created by the Ministry of International Trade and Industry. Many individuals stamped their approval before it reached the final decision-maker, the minister.]] A '''''Ringi-sho''''' (稟議書)According to [[Kōjien]] and [[Daijirin]], the reading of "稟議書" is "Ringi-sho." However, various Japanese dictionaries classify "Ringi" as a [[Hyakushō-yomi|popular reading]] (''hyakushō-yomi'') of "Hingi." is a document created for the purpose of conducting ''[[:en:Ringi|Ringi]]'' (consultative decision-making). In [[:en:government agencies|government agencies]], it is often referred to as a '''''Kiansho''''' (起案書), while in corporate settings, alternative terms like '''''Ringi-sho,'' ''Kiansho,'' and ''Ritsuansho''''' may also be used. == Overview == A ''Ringi-sho'' is a document used for conducting ''[[:en:Ringi|Ringi]]'', a method within organizations such as [[:en:companies|companies]] and government agencies to reduce the time spent convening [[:en:meetings|meetings]]. In this method, the person in charge drafts a proposal for a straightforward matter and circulates it among stakeholders to obtain their seals or signatures of approval. In principle, organizational decisions are made through a {{ill|consensus decision-making|en|consensus decision-making|WD=Q188577|lt=consensus decision-making|1=Consensus decision-making}} process like meetings. For instance, decisions about executing business operations, such as those made by a {{ill|board of directors|en|board of directors|WD=Q188628|lt=board of directors|1=Board of directors}}, must be performed by the company's directors themselves, and circular resolutions are only allowed exceptionally. However, meetings consume time and costs, so day-to-day decisions and simpler approvals are often delegated to designated decision-makers. Nonetheless, entrusting all judgment to the final decision-maker could risk neglecting due diligence on individual cases. Furthermore, securing approval from involved parties before making a decision ensures smooth execution post-decision. ''Ringi'' facilitates a broader and more cautious consideration by involving multiple stakeholders before the final decision-maker grants approval. Additionally, documenting the content and process of organizational decision-making in written form is desirable not only for operational convenience but also to make auditing and external review easier. When decisions are made in meetings, meeting minutes or summaries of resolutions are created and recorded. Conversely, when decisions are made via ''Ringi'', the person initiating the proposal prepares a document detailing the proposed decision, which is circulated for stakeholders’ approvals, eventually forming the ''Ringi-sho''. The final decision-maker confirms stakeholders' approvals before authorizing the proposal, completing the organization's decision-making process. This method is particularly suited for decision-making in relatively large organizations. == Format == The format of a ''Ringi-sho'' varies between organizations. It is often an internal document, with each organization establishing its own format. Below is an example: {{Indent| (Title) Regarding the purchase of a company vehicle
(Body) Concerning the above matter, I propose the purchase of a vehicle as outlined below:
Details
# Reason: The delivery vehicle has deteriorated, necessitating a replacement.
# Cost: '''*,000 yen }} Additional details such as the vendor's name, the budget allocated (e.g., ''' yen), actual expenses (e.g., ''' yen), and any differences are typically included, along with an attached quotation. In cases involving purchasing goods, many organizations use a certain monetary threshold above which a ''Ringi-sho'' is required. Additionally, ''Ringi-sho'' are used for a wide range of organizational approvals, such as [[personnel changes]], promotions, and contract agreements. The process involves the initiator of the proposal, their immediate supervisor, and ultimately the decision-maker stamping or signing the document. Decision-making authority is often based on monetary thresholds—for example, contracts exceeding a certain amount may require the president's approval. == Storage and Management of ''Ringi-sho'' == As internal documents, ''Ringi'' and ''Ringi-sho'' are often stored and managed by departments such as [[general affairs]]. In Japan, there is no legal mandate specifying retention periods for typical ''Ringi-sho''. Organizations independently determine retention periods in their internal regulations. == Audit of ''Ringi-sho'' == During [[:en:Internal controls|Internal controls]] evaluations or operational audits, ''Ringi-sho'' may be included as part of the review process. This involves verifying that the approvals were granted by appropriate decision-makers as per authority regulations, that the ''Ringi'' contents were properly documented, and that the documents are systematically organized and stored. Furthermore, during [[financial audits]] or [[tax inspections]], ''Ringi-sho'' may be reviewed to understand the decision-making processes and transaction purposes underlying financial and tax treatments. == Notes == {{notelist|qq=}} == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == See Also == * [[:en:Internal controls|Internal controls]] * [[General affairs]] {{translated page|ja|稟議書|version=96919592|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:稟議書]] {{wikidata link|Q11596356}} [[Category:Corporate governance]] [[Category:Japanese administration]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]
